summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Left hand pain

History of Present Illness
The patient reports pain in the left hand, stating that it started this morning and is worsening. He describes the pain as sharp and localized, with a sensation of tearing. The patient states he was cycling for approximately 45 minutes to an hour this morning and feels tightness in his leg. He reports pain on the right side. He also reports pain in the left side. The patient reports pain in the right leg. The patient reports pain in the calf. The patient reports soreness in his leg. The patient runs approximately 10 kilometers.

Findings
Examination reveals tenderness to palpation in the left hand. No swelling or range of motion limitations are observed. Vital signs are within normal limits.

Assessment
Achilles tendinitis

Plan
The doctor will discharge the patient and schedule a follow-up appointment in 7 to 10 days. The doctor orders physical therapy visits. The doctor recommends taking ibuprofen. The doctor asks about the patient's stretching routine. The doctor asks about the patient's running routine. The doctor asks about the patient's swimming routine.
reference_summary:
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Dennis Martin is a pleasant 46-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right heel pain. The onset of his pain began 10 days ago while training for a triathlon. He locates his pain to the posterior aspect of his heel, which radiates proximally into his calf. His pain is aggravated by working out and when he first gets up in the morning. If he sits for a long period, his pain will return upon standing. The patient states that he runs 10 kilometers in the morning. At lunchtime, he tries to get on a bike for about 45 minutes to an hour. In the evening, he goes to the aquatic center and swims for 30 minutes to 1 hour. The patient states that he runs 5 days per week. He adds that he alternates between a long run and a long bike ride on weekends. He currently takes one day off from his exercises. The patient states that he does not stretch at all. He denies any left leg pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right heel pain. Denies left leg pain.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the right heel: Pain with resisted extension. Mild pain with flexion. Positive calcaneal squeeze test. Severe tenderness to the Achilles tendon. Thompson test is negative, bilaterally.

RESULTS

No new imaging was obtained today

ASSESSMENT

Right Achilles tendinitis.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination today, I had a lengthy discussion with the patient regarding his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his symptoms are likely secondary to overuse. I have recommended that we treat the patient conservatively. The patient was instructed to take at least 1 week off from his training to recover from this. I will provide him with a referral for formal physical therapy to strengthen his right lower extremities. I have advised him to take 3 tablets of ibuprofen every 6 hours for pain. If his pain does not improve with physical therapy, I will recommend obtaining an x-ray.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 7 to 10 days to check on his progress.

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Left hand pain
History of Present Illness
The patient reports pain in the left hand, stating that it started this morning and is worsening. He describes the pain as sharp and localized, with a sensation of tearing. The patient states he was cycling for approximately 45 minutes to an hour this morning and feels tightness in his leg. He reports pain on the right side. He also reports pain in the left side. The patient reports pain in the right leg. The patient reports pain in the calf. The patient reports soreness in his leg. The patient runs approximately 10 kilometers.
Findings
Examination reveals tenderness to palpation in the left hand. No swelling or range of motion limitations are observed. Vital signs are within normal limits.
Assessment
Achilles tendinitis
Plan
The doctor will discharge the patient and schedule a follow-up appointment in 7 to 10 days. The doctor orders physical therapy visits. The doctor recommends taking ibuprofen. The doctor asks about the patient's stretching routine. The doctor asks about the patient's running routine. The doctor asks about the patient's swimming routine.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pain in the left hand, stating that it started this morning and is worsening. He describes the pain as sharp and localized, with a sensation of tearing.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The HPI focuses almost entirely on left hand pain, but the Assessment lists Achilles tendinitis, which pertains to leg/calf symptoms. The descriptive pain statements about the left hand do not support the listed diagnosis and are inconsistent with it, creating a misleading linkage between section content and diagnostic impression.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient states he was cycling for approximately 45 minutes to an hour this morning and feels tightness in his leg. He reports pain on the right side. He also reports pain in the left side. The patient reports pain in the right leg. The patient reports pain in the calf. The patient reports soreness in his leg. The patient runs approximately 10 kilometers.
section_miscategorizations.expected_section: History of Present Illness and Assessment
section_miscategorizations.reason: These statements appropriately belong in HPI as patient-reported symptoms and activity history, but given the diagnosis of Achilles tendinitis, some synthesis of these leg/calf complaints should also appear in the Assessment. The absence of any leg/calf symptom summary in the Assessment creates a mild organizational mismatch between HPI and Assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor asks about the patient's stretching routine. The doctor asks about the patient's running routine. The doctor asks about the patient's swimming routine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are history-taking questions, not future-oriented interventions or instructions. They describe information-gathering during the encounter rather than a plan of care, so placing them in the Plan section is misleading.
